Hey, this is Sean and Andre. It's week four of the Art of Email workshop. Today we are going to be talking about the product launch series, customer onboarding series, and putting it all together module in the Art of Email. We're going to start off with product launch series. This week might be a little bit different rather than ask very specific questions in sort of this is more like week one where we just have some placeholders and we're just going to riff about some ideas for each of these modules. These are three every module in the Art of Email is important, but these are three really important modules that the conversation and we're both kind of curious to see where the conversation is going to lead. We've been talking about this for about 40 minutes already this morning and realize that we probably should click record. So, Andre, where do you want to start on the PLS? That seems like a good place to get going. Yeah. Yeah, because it can be quite nuanced. I like the idea of which we've mentioned before, I think, the bullets to cannonballs idea where it's a perfect vehicle for for testing out bullets, you know, for exposing segments of an audience to an idea and seeing if that idea has legs, if it resonates and if it's worth doubling down and actually turning it into reality, which is the cannonball component. So we've done a number of these, at least during our collaboration. And yeah, they're always slightly different, but they're always fun because we always learning something. So maybe that's a good jumping off point. And just I know that we talked about this in the past, but if someone's just jumping in here at this point, we'll just do a quick overview. This is bullets to cannonballs is an idea from Jim Collins, a business author. And the idea is that you, you know, I think the metaphor he uses if you're on a ship and you have a certain amount of gunpowder and a ship is approaching and you have just enough to fire a cannonball, which if you think the other ship, you're safe. And if you don't, you're going to get sunk in what Jim, the metaphor that Collins uses is that rather than just load up the cannon, fire the cannonball, hope for the best, that you fire a few bullets first, use a little bit of the gunpowder, fire a few bullets to orient where the cannon is going to fire. And then once you once you get to orient to the get your aim right, then you put the rest of the powder behind the cannonball and you fire and sink the other ship. That's the metaphor of bullets to cannonballs. And we've we embrace this. And if you think about it from a PLS perspective, bullets to cannonballs could be if you have an idea for an offer, which we did earlier in 2021, we had this idea for something we called the one that's in the workshop, lean addition. It didn't exist prior to the promotion itself. We were very clear about that. And it was just an idea. And so the the the if we if we had a cannonball version, would have been to create the entire workshop, cross our fingers, hope that our audience was interested in it and then try to sell it. And if we were wrong, then we would have put all of the effort into creating the the workshop and would have missed. So what we did instead, and this is a way to this is the way to use this metaphor. Think about using the PLS is we we were very clear with cohort one that this did not exist. It would be delivered in a live environment very much like this part of the email workshop. The first time was delivered as a live workshop. And we had no idea we we want to say we had no idea. We suspected there was a segment of our audience interested in a live workshop or a workshop companion to the art of the email, but we didn't know that for certain. So rather than create all of it in advance, that's the cannonball. We sold it first and then and we sold it with everybody's eyes wide open that it would be delivered over five weeks. And that was the bullet that then gave us a signal back and said, OK, this is something that makes sense. And there's part of our audience that are interested that's interested in it. So that metaphor, the PLS is a really great tool to test the bullets versus cannonballs idea, the bullets to cannonballs. By again, as long as you're honest, you're saying this thing doesn't exist or this is this is how it's going to exist over this time frame. It's a PLS is a really great way to test an idea. You can add to that. And sort of do you want to this is probably a good segue into talking about the moment to build your workshops. We've run two cohorts for it now. We're going to run a third cohort early in two thousand twenty two. You want to riff on that a little bit and sort of how those things differ. And I know that's going to open the door to this idea of a hybrid. And there's just lots to talk about on the PLS. I'm going to kind of follow your lead on where we go next. Yeah, so, I mean. So as Sean said, we've we we put out the moment of bloody workshop cohort one early in the year. We then did a cohort two. So we really knew that this is something that people wanted. So cohort two. And so it was still open and close. And we had made changes to the course, upgraded certain components of it. And so it's it it it lives in this in this world between evergreen and open and close, whereas the course itself is created, but we keep adding to it and upgrading it. But it's it's an open close deal. So the PLS is is a perfect vehicle to get people that are interested in whichever one is to raise their hand and then come into it. We have an idea of what we need to say at this point within the PLS. So there's less there's less learning that we need to do and more just making sure that our PLS is is is as good as it possibly can. And maybe try and figure out a few new things components. But then there's also an opportunity for us to take that PLS and turn it into something that's more automated, yet still open and close. So you can head down that avenue at some point. Do you have any? Two thoughts come two thoughts come out of that. What you just said, I mean, first. And a first thought is that, you know, we if you look at the art of email and you look at a story, power promotion and PLS, it's two discrete things. I mean, sure, that's true. All right. So the general distinction between the two is that story, power, promotion or for things that's something that's evergreen. And then PLS is for something that has an hard open or close. But rather than seeing those as two discrete things, I think there's value in seeing them as as ends of a spectrum. Right. And where you where you are on that spectrum mixes, you know, can mix and match elements of the two in that. And I'll have you talk about that in a second. I'm going to hit one other point before we jump into that. And I just go through this module if I'm doing if the certain criteria are true. Like it's there's a hard open and close. So it's always a PLS versus it's evergreen and it's always a story, power, promotion. Well, that's that's not how that when you once you understand the basics, you realize that those those are two ends of a spectrum that's somewhat you're going to blend some of these things. And we can give an example before we do that. I just want to mention that a PLS is also it's the vehicle for businesses that have open and closed enrollment along with the hybrid that Andre is going to discuss in a moment. But. And this is important. It's it's in some of the things we were talking about before we click record was how the Internet goes through cycles and and always have. And I assume it always will. So, you know, early on the the when Jeff Walker sort of released his even before he released product launch formula and product launch became a thing that sort of became part of the vernacular even before that happened as it was happening. There was just a lot of excitement around the product launch idea. And then, of course, like every any situation, there's a lot of excitement about it. There are a lot of courses sold or a lot of people who spoke about the product launch like it was gospel. And then everybody did product launches. And of course, then the pendulum started to swing back the other way because, you know, and then lots of people who built their businesses around the product launch model wanted to get off the product launch train. And it's because there's those things if you just open and close enrollment once or twice a year, three times four times, there's a lot of work. And there is there's just there's it creates a set of demands. And lots of people wanted to do less of that. And now we're at this. We're seeing it. So then lots. Then we move to much more emphasis on evergreen every certainly several years ago, that was the buzz where everybody wanted to move all of their offers evergreen. And now we're starting to see the pendulum swing swinging back again. And we have there are a lot of there are a lot of platforms that offer this. And there are a lot of a lot of courses available now where the emphasis is on the cohort model. So that's an open and closed enrollment. And there are cohorts who go through each model. Now, you know, depending on when you're listening to this in the future, the pendulum may and it's never absolute. It's you know, but it's the sort of the zeitgeist, the gestalt of the market, like where it's leaning. And right now it's starting to swing back toward the open and closed cohort based model because of that. If you're if you're entering the art of email and your business is based on an open and closed enrollment model, the PLS really is where to focus your attention. And it doesn't mean you don't get the benefits of the story powered promotion. When Andre starts talking about how we can use a hybrid of those two. That's part of it. But start if you're listening to this and your business has open and closed enrollment, start thinking about your next PLS. That's really in yours, you know, it'd be specific to your business, their duration, all of those things, but that's really where to focus. That's the primary mover in an open and closed enrollment business. And I hope that sets the stage for this hybrid model. So why don't you give us the overview of the hybrid model? And then I'll just ask questions as they come up. Yeah. So a hybrid could be it's, you know, it's a hybrid model. It's using the leverage of that you get inside of an evergreen. So inside of a story, a power promotion and but still recognizing that the thing that you promoting is still open and close. So which means there's a specific date it's going to the doors are going to open, the car is going to open and you take orders and then it's going to shut down. And I think most most decent ESPs. We use the convert kit. You can you can specify dates so you can you can have a story powered promotion that is triggered at certain so emails are sent out at certain on certain days. So if we know that, you know, Monday is a certain day and that's what let's just say that enrollment is going to open on a Friday and it's going to close on a Monday. Well, you can have a follow up sequence that that knows that. And so it's triggered on a certain date. So therefore, all the emails inside of it are delivered on on the correct days. And so you kind of get the best of both worlds. You can. But again, this really needs to come later. I wouldn't, you know, start off doing a evergreen PLS because that defeats the object of why you do these things and laugh. So you can you can learn all the stuff. Yeah, I was just thinking about I mean, there's so many ways, so many nuances to this and something you just said, I want to really draw attention to, which is don't try to do the ten times advanced version first. And this is a mistake we all make. So, you know, there's that whole idea when you point a finger at somebody else for pointing back at yourself kind of thing. This is me pointing at this problem and pointing it at myself as well, which is it's really easy to to take something like the PLS or story powered promotion and then really complicate them to the point of making it, making it challenging. So don't do that. I mean, really get comfortable with the PLS first as sort of as written and get comfortable with the story powered promotion as written before you start thinking about how these hybrids overlap. And really the hybrid. The hybrid is probably the natural evolution of the two. So and when we did the second one, cohort two, I think we borrowed a little bit from that first one, of course, because we're describing and we're telling the story of the original one. And so there's there's a natural story there. But what I would imagine could happen at some point is that the the and this is true. And if you think about when this would be true. You know, ideally, and I know I'm going to shock some people, but there's a pretty good chance that the first thing you write is not going to be the best thing. You're right. I know everybody's stunned when they hear that. That's true for us, certainly. Right. So you might do what's just, you know, PLS version one, right? You do it at a point in time. You get a certain result. And there are things that you look back on that you thought now it's pretty good. And then there are things that didn't work as well. And then version two, you make it better. Right. But, you know, by versions three and beyond, you kind of you begin to have a sense of what worked, what resonated, what didn't resonate, perhaps. A lot of the most important things to say have been said. So say them the way they said them. You know, in their cup, I'll just one last I'll show you on this that I made a note when you were talking of is that. You know, I know one thing that we we've approached this in two different ways, you know, sometimes we link to things that we've already written previous emails, for example. And other times we embed what we've already written into an email. And we reference that this was written for something in the past. You know, like we might say something as simple as, you know, as we've said in a previous email, and then we just put the text in there. There are lots of ways to do this. And I think when you write something that's that resonates, pay attention to that signal and keep reusing it. Don't set yourself up for a situation where you're every time you're creating a PLS, you're trying to come up with some new way to resonate with your audience, unless you haven't yet. But once you start to, that's that's a good place to borrow liberally from yourself, because it's that that's valuable to your audience. It's it's it's been shown to be valuable in the previous audiences. So continue to to to extract the value that you've already created. Yeah, that's that's how I think about it. I don't know if there's a wander around too much there at all. Yeah, I'll just add one more thing. And then I suppose we've we've we've murdered it. Well, you can you can modify that that thing that's just played out now, because now it's it's it's played out. No one's going to go through it again until it gets modified and then made love for the next time you run a cohort based enrollment. So you can modify that thing on the postmortem, where it's where it's the most fresh in your mind, very mind, you didn't have to interact with a with a complete PLS, which, you know, that's that's a that's a huge time resource. So you freed up time because it's now mostly automated. But then once once once people are in, then you think of like a few ideas that you could use to improve it for next time around, also based on on feedback. And then, you know, for next time around, you've got something that was made better, modified slightly. So it's not the exact same thing. Should somebody decide to raise the hand and go through it again? And while, in fact, if somebody's gone through it already, at least this is how we do it. People get laughter maxis. So they'll never have to go through another. Harvard or PLS or SDP of of an open and close, unless they didn't respond to it and join. All right, let's move on to the customer on boarding series. We've talked to I think we feel like we've talked about this a lot, but I can't remember if we've already talked about it and recorded it. So we there may be some duplication. And, you know, the marathon Q&A sessions, we, you know, maybe where some of the shows up to. So I'll kick this one off just a compare and contrast. I won't name the products. They're both sitting on my desk here. And it just the experiences of buying both of those. These are both hard goods. But I think there's a lesson in that purchase that just to me, it really speaks to this idea of a customer on boarding series. So one, what I mean, this is sort of what not to do versus but to do from my perspective as a customer. One of them, when I made the purchase, immediately, before it was even delivered, I was getting the, you know, buy the next thing 20% off. Do you have this buy, buy, buy, buy? And I really, and I completely tuned out like all of their communication very quickly because I had just purchased. So, and this is not, it's funny. This is not at all uncommon. And I'm sure I have some vague memories of hearing at different times. And when someone is made a purchase, they're more likely to make a purchase soon after. Like they're in that. So, you know, go for the purchase. That to me seems like bad advice. Like, and maybe, you know, maybe this is true in some cases. And, you know, again, I'm just speaking about my own experience. But this one product buying it and then getting hammered with discounts to buy more, and especially in a way that it didn't really make sense for me to buy more. And I hadn't received the, it was being encouraged to buy more before I even had the initial purchase in my hands. It just, it didn't, it felt really gross. And I just very quickly tuned out all communication from that seller. And now I have this other thing on my desk where I can't, I think I signed up. I guess I purchased that first. And then after purchasing it, before I got it, but the email, I got a what to expect email that sort of talked about the, you know, where it was made, the materials that it's made from, why they chose those materials. So before the thing arrived, I was really like looking forward to opening it because I knew the story about it and was excited about it. At some point after I, I think it was after I received it, I got another notification that essentially said that because I had made this purchase that I could get a, I think it was a 20% discount on future refills or something, whatever it was, it was just mentioned in passing at the end. I didn't really have to do anything. And then also mentioned that each month there was a customer only offer that was sent out. But that email didn't have the customer only offer. Just kind of let me know that that was coming. So later when an email, I noticed myself as emails were coming in, I was curious, what's the customer offer this week, this month? It was a very different vibe between those two. And it's, the point I'm making here is not so much to look at either one of these as the right way versus the wrong way, but to point to this larger truth that how we interact with our customers after a sale is very impactful. If someone just did the thing that we want them to do, they became a paying customer. And it's so tempting to just figure, oh, shoot, good. Now let's go see if I can get another one. Right. And it's, that's very short-sighted. Or let's see if I can get some more money out of this person, right? They just thought maybe they'll buy more. That minds that there are consequences to that mindset. And the question that we have to ask ourselves is, are we, do we want a business that's subject to those particular consequences? Or would we rather create different consequences? And if we have this long-term focus and we're not concerned about getting every sale and striving for every sale at every moment, and we can relax a little because we know that the sales will emerge as a result of the relationship that we're building, that it suggests at least to me that once someone has become a customer, the place to put most of our attention is on how they, how their experience as a customer can be improved, how that can be excellent by drawing attention to the decision that they just made and how they can get the most valuable. And really treating that relationship, that relationship very, very well. It's part, it's part thank you, it's part, it just, there's so much there. But the last thing that feels right to me and that is to try to hammer away at another purchase as the default. And that seems to be, there seems to be an undercurrent of that that we run into. So I'll hand it over to you to say something smart on that one rather than just me, my experience. Yeah, I mean, I see this a lot with with e-commerce. I suppose this isn't, this isn't the place to, to poke sticks at certain people's business models, but it's certainly easier to do, I suppose, with e-commerce where, you know, reasons to send emails are discounts and the pushing discounts all the time, every month. I suppose, you know, people that are selling information like, like we do. Although I'm sure it's not exclusive for this. You know, I haven't got an e-commerce business, but you know, I can, I can think of how I would perhaps do something. And I don't see why narrative based emails can't, you know, like sending out these custom onboarding series that, that take somebody on a little journey about this thing that they've just purchased, even if it's not information, which is probably easier to do. Just thinking, I mean, there's, there's so many products that are, that I get that are, you know, stuff like creamers and MCT oils and stuff like that. But, you know, there's no reason why you can't make that experience an enjoyable one as opposed to just being a transaction. Yeah, otherwise, I think that module is, it's, it's fairly straightforward. Someone's just made a purchase. You want them to have the best possible experience they can have and doing it in that moment is the best time to do it. I mean, that's when their attention is peaked, right? And someone just made a purchase there. They're interested right in general, they're interested right now. I mean, I've certainly purchased things when I wasn't interested right now, but certainly around like Black Friday and other stuff and all the discounts happen. Sometimes you make a bunch of purchases and so there it's not always, it's not always true. But in general, I think it's true that you have somebody's attention right now and it's yours to lose. And yours to screw up. And it seems like there are an infinite number of ways that based on my inbox to screw it up and only a few ways to do it right. But if you, it's like, it just seems to me like if you strip away, if you look at all of the things that can screw it up have in common, and then you compare that to the few ways not to screw it up, look at what that those few ways have in common. It seems like the two ends of the spectrum are something around this assumption that because someone just purchased, you could squeeze another purchase out of them right now versus recognizing that someone just purchased. And that's really important. They just took a step toward you and focusing on rewarding that, knowing that in the short term, that will reduce refund rates. So there's no downside to building a relationship after somebody becomes a customer. Like I just, I can't see any downside unless we have this fear based mentality that if we don't get that next sale as fast as possible, we're going to lose the opportunity forever. And that's just the way it is. And I think that's the way that we're going to do it. And I think that's the way that we're going to do it. And I think that's the way that we're going to do it. And I think that's the way that we're going to do it. And that's just not true. I don't know. That's sort of how I think about the customer onboarding series. Yeah, totally. I think when somebody makes, you know, broadly speaking, when somebody makes a purchase, their level of excitement is at the highest. They've just made their purchase. And we all love buying stuff. So, yeah, I don't know if we've getting close to murdering that now. Interesting choice of words there. And I guess we'll wrap it up today when we'll talk about module 10, which is putting it all together. We talked about this earlier, I hope we can recreate and expand on the conversation that we already have. So, let's do a little ping pong, I guess. I'll hand it off to you. Why don't you talk about how you think about going through the art of email. Because we know it from the inside out. So, knowing what we know, how would you approach going through the art of email? And then I'll just sort of wherever you get to with that, I'll run with it because I have no idea what I'm going to add to that yet, which is always entertaining for me. Yeah, I mean, so we all learn slightly differently, but I think one thing's in common. If you don't understand something like you don't have that understanding of the big picture, how all the parts fit together, how they can interact and how they can produce the result, which is an emerging property of making this thing, you know, a reality. So, it's, we think that the optimal flow to go through art of email is from module one all the way through to module 10 in order. Maybe some modules you would have to reread a few times, but not to the level where you're trying to think about how to put something. How to put something. What is the underlying technology required to make this thing, you know, how to put it into my system? I think the better way to do it is just to get that initial understanding of how the whole thing works. The putting it all together module 10, I think that's where the main focus would be because I think most people, no one's going to have the same path into this, you know, as to what they're going to build for themselves. Maybe most people will build a relationship building series, but that's not going to be the case for every single person. So, you know, just figuring out what your priorities are in that putting it all together module, and then that would then kick off. Okay, so now I've decided I'm going to do do relationship building series and then to the newsletter and then it's like, okay, now I can deep dive on those modules required to bring the thing to life and then, you know, go from there instead of it's so easy for people to get hung up on something that's not relevant in this moment because it may not be something that you need to build, you know, in this moment. I think the story part promotions can seem overwhelming when you look at it, but you know, maybe most people aren't going to need to build one of those for a while. So don't worry about them. Fill up on looking back and forward. Yes. So do you have any response to that. First thing I should probably do is unmute myself. I mean the thing that struck me with putting it all together. And this continues to be something that I. I, it's ephemeral because I see it was such clarity but then when I try to grab a hold of it it's, I can't quite get my hands on it. I think what it, but it's something around. It's really around two things and I know we talked about using the module itself but it's really around an experience I had with with complexity was systemic complexity that I think we shared in the module itself. And then it's knowing that the art of email was designed where all of the parts are so that they're all part of the same system. So, and that sounds obvious, but it's, it's, it's not obvious because it's entirely possible. We could have written and created an email marketing course that had modules on parts. We could pick and choose the parts and like and get a result from the parts. But that when you put the parts together, like the result might have been incrementally better. But the art of email is different in that we, the parts actually emerge from the whole. And like the parts are the ways to express the whole. And that sounds, and I realize that sounds like like we're hyper intellectualizing something but it's really at the core of what makes the art of email so powerful is because when, when all of the parts are based on the same principles that they're, they're crafted from the same I systemic ideas to produce that they're sort of based, they're based on the same foundation, because that's true. Every time something gets added to it, the whole system changes, the whole system with not reacts to that there is improved by that. And at first, that's not, it's not really noticeable because the effect size is small. So if you have a relationship building series that's going to change things dramatically for your business. You'll feel that we've already seen, we've already had people submitting their initial results and they're stunned. So we know that's true, but that's a, that's a, that's creating one thing that's not really a system that's dropping one, one part of the art of email into an existing business system, and it's, and it's having these are seismic effects. And then when, when you, if you assume that it's after your relationship building series, you add a value newsletter, like that, those two things now interact together, because in ways that are obvious and ways that are not obvious, like the obvious way is someone goes to a relationship building series and then they get this newsletter and it's been like the newsletter is framed by the building of the relationship first. So that some of those things are obvious, but then somebody may get a value newsletter for 12 months and not explicitly remember anything about the relationship building series, but their opinion of the newsletter was improved right from the beginning because of that. So there's like all this weird stuff that happens, but where it gets really fascinating to me is that because the parts are designed to be to work together. Each time we add something to it. The, we increase the likelihood of, of a, of an exponential change. Charlie Munger, Warren Buffett's business partner called these Lollipalooza effects. It's like when you get a lot, you know, like all of a sudden something massive happens with the addition of something small, seemingly comparatively small, but the, the addition of the comparatively small things happen to be in line with lots of other things or a contact or something happens. And this one small thing creates this massive effect but it's not the small thing that created the massive effect. It's the small thing dropped into the right context and the right systemic output. So I, the art of email. That was the thing that really struck me as we, as we kept digging it and tearing pieces apart and reassembling and structuring it the way that we did. It seems like in retrospect it was always in service to this idea that this needed to be the irreducible expression of email marketing like you can't take anything away without it causing significant damage to the whole. Like if you just pulled something out of the art of email, like add and it doesn't mean you have to do everything, but it means that there would be there's some there's there people out there who they everybody, you know, when we look at the audience as a whole everybody needs all of it. There's nothing that can be removed and how in the art of email is okay you don't necessarily in your context need all of it. But all of it has to be there and at the same time, there's nothing more to add to it that would improve it if we added something else, we would likely make it worse. And that that that striking that balance so that's, you know, this isn't sort of a long circuitous description and I think the other thing that really reference the personal experience I had working, working on a team of 16 that had scaled from a team of four. And I'm not, you know, we joke a lot that five out of four people are bad at math, Andre and are not mathematicians. But it's funny how our brains deceive us because you know I remember thinking to myself, like, why is this going so sideways 16 is only four times bigger than four. I don't remember who drew my attention to this idea that it's not that it and I, but I do remember being part of that team when, when someone pointed out or I somehow stumbled on this idea that because teams are about the number of people on the teams who you can interact with. It's the interactions that matter, not the parts, and a 16 person team is four times more complex than a four person team. But when you look at the interactions is 20 times more complex, because that's just the way the math works. So when you think about that, that metaphor has been stuck in my head as we've been working on the art of email because what it points to is that every time you add a layer to your art of email inspired email system, you are, you're creating the opportunities for exponential complexity to emerge because of the relationships, which gets back to this foundational idea that it was designed to work together. And I haven't, I just, I haven't figured out a way to express this any more succinctly than that but it's those two forces working together to me that like that's so profoundly powerful. And getting back to what you said, Andre about, you know, functionally, like, go through all of it, understand that all of it's there for a reason, although you may not need all of it, but you will probably need most of it, and your responsibility once you understand all the pieces is to then get in module 10, get some perspective and try to decide where's your, where's your entry point. What do you do first, and then just continue to reorient to that now that you've done that what next, and now that you've done that and that what do you do next and, and at some point, the effect size begins to change dramatically. You do one thing you get a small result, you do another thing you get a small result, you do another thing to result is a little more significant but at some point you do something, and then the result is far outsized what you would expect, because the whole system is now producing that result. I don't know if that makes any sense at all, make sense in my head which scares me. I suspect that makes any sense. I suspect that there's that there's no business that can't implement out of email in its entirety, even if they think that their business doesn't need all the, all the parts as we describe in the course so for example, the first thing that comes to mind is. If somebody has a co what based business and open and close affair so it's easy, and that's it. You know it's a small business, they do not have to close a few times a year. That's everything. So I think it's easy to come to conclusion that well maybe you don't need to do any story powered promotions in that context. But I think there's, there's many opportunities to do in this example story powered promotions, you could do them around affiliate offers for example, so you know just again just thinking out larger for for a sec. So we could, Sean and I could write an entire story powered promotion around the hosting company that we've decided to use, and then we can reflect back on previous hosting companies that we've used in the 40 plus years of, you know, jointly. We could probably jump on a call with with the company that we use and asking to do a zoom call with us for, for 40 minutes and unpack while you know certain things and we could really make that story powered promotion, like world class and it, you know, maybe it plays out over, over seven emails over a week. I don't do open and close. Well, you know, there's, there's still ways to to use a PLS as a bullet, for example, to to to learn something about an audience. Before it's turned into the, the cannonball version and something else happens so. Yeah. That's, I think that's, you know, it's worth mentioning the fact that I think most most people could implement what can implement the model of email in its entirety over time you know where it makes sense based on priorities. And it reminds me of the Michael Lewis book the undoing project where I think it's a reference to Danny Conneman as a professor. And I traveled my previous career I traveled to the Middle East a lot traveled to Israel many times and the economy describes sort of this. The confrontational nature of sort of Israeli intellectual thought in the university. And I one of my dear friends is Israeli and I just remember in graduate school very similar like this this. It's almost a combative it's not a mean spirit combative but it's a combative intellectual jousting that happens but with Conneman it said to his graduate students, something to the effect of when someone says something don't try to figure out what's wrong with it try to figure out what ways it could be right and what situations it could be right. And it's an interesting way to think about the art of email to it's very, it's very easy to look at something that doesn't fit my business and try to move on. Take a page from Danny Conneman's book and you know take a step back and say okay and what situations could this make sense for me. What ways could I use this and I think that what you just described as an example of that there's, and you asked that question, it's just a better question you get better answers. And I think that question is a really powerful that when you might look at something like our newsletter just or our business just doesn't need to use like it doesn't benefit from that it's different because of xyz well. What ways could a newsletter be helpful. Because when you ask that question. It doesn't mean the answers you gather going to convince you to do it, but at least it's going to give you better answers. And I think because we didn't. You know, it's not just enough to say that we didn't include any fluff in the art of email that's that's an understatement. It's precise in a way that it's systemically precise like it. It's really. We agonized over a lot of decisions and a lot of what the final what exists in the final product is not what it looked like in the in its original form because it was a constant hammering against like how could this be better. And how could we you know some parts of it now are separate and other parts are the star kept together in ways that was different than the original because of that that mindset that that desire to make it so precise. So I think that'll be my last thought I'm putting it all together is realize the parts are all there because of how they relate to one another. So if the part itself, you look at it and think I don't know that this really fits for my business recognize that it's not just the part that matters. It's the relationship of that part to everything else. And there's no better example of that than the relationship building series that has such profound effects on everything else and embracing that mindset of building relationships before transactions is at the core of everything that the art of the email is about and you can. It's very it's it's very easy to misinterpret that to be a decision that we made because that's just the kind of business that we want to have. That happens to be true, but it's not. That's that's not the only reason that we made that decision. It happens to be true that we want to have a relationship build a relationship based business with our audience, but it also happens to be true that most people who are going to buy from you are not going to buy from you. And immediately, first 90 days or so that's fact that's gravity. So the knowing that those two things exist simultaneously and independently. We want to have a business this way and we're lucky because it just so happens that we want to have a business that lines up perfectly with how reality works. But if we didn't want to have a business to emphasize relationships, it would not change the fact that most people who would buy from us wouldn't buy from us in the first 90 days. We can't change that reality where the art of email is really it's it's based fundamentally on that reality and you can approach that reality from a. Touchy feel you perspective like this is the way I want to have my business for or from a hard nose economic decision financial perspective where the opportunity the financial opportunity in your audience is past 90 days. That's between 90 days and two years is where overwhelming opportunity is financially. And if if you just want a hard nose financial look at email marketing, that's where to focus your attention. So there's you're not giving up anything in the art of email by embracing its philosophy. When you put it all together. It simply is an expression of the reality of the way the world works in a way that we believe is the best to take advantage of that reality. So that'll be my closing thought I'm putting it all together. Yeah, I like that on that bombshell. I think we became for about an hour now so. Oh nice.
